FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study of 9 to 12-year-old adolescents in the United States; N=8267

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multiple linear regression models examining prospective and cross-sectional associations between screen time and nutrition

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Academic Pediatrics, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 identified phone/SM-specific estimates: television (β=-0.14 and -0.07), video games (β=-0.15 and -0.16), videos/YouTube (β=-0.18 and -0.24), texting (β=-0.15), and social media (β=-0.16) all paired with MIND diet nutrition score. Nutrition is individual-trait-level (behavioral health outcome)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple β coefficients reported: television -0.14 to -0.07, video games -0.15 to -0.16, videos -0.18 to -0.24, texting -0.15, social media -0.16 (95% CI reported for all)

FT_IC4.evidence: Linear regression with reported β coefficients and 95% confidence intervals for SM/phone variables paired with nutrition outcome; quantitative results throughout

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional carve-out applies: paper reports both prospective (Year 0→Year 1) and cross-sectional (Year 1) analyses with SM as DV. Cross-sectional SM-as-DV analysis exists, disqualifying EC7 regardless of longitudinal component
notes: Clear Step 1 hit on FT-IC2. All phone/SM variables measured separately. MIND diet nutrition is well-established individual-trait outcome. Paper includes both prospective and cross-sectional design.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD Study®); N=9859, Year 3 (2019–2021), ages 12–13 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multiple linear regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Annals of Epidemiology, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines associations between gender identity (transgender/gender-questioning vs. cisgender) as IV and screen use variables (television/movies, video games, texting, social media) as DV. This reverses the IC2 requirement: phone/SM must be paired with individual-trait on the OTHER side. Here phone/SM is the DV and gender identity is the IV. Gender identity qualifies as individual-trait when it is the outcome, but the paper uses it as a predictor (IV). The DV is screen time, not an individual-trait outcome. Does not meet IC2 specification that phone/SM variable pair with individual-trait on the other side

notes: Paper's analytic direction (gender identity→screen use) does not match IC2 requirement. Screen time is outcome variable, not exposure. Gender identity as predictor does not qualify as individual-trait on the other side per IC2 mechanical rules.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (ABCD Study); Years 2–3, 2018–2021; N=9,398 early adolescents aged 11–12 years

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis using logistic, ordinal logistic, and linear regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 identified phone/SM-specific estimates: texting and social media (paired with sleep disturbance and sleep duration outcomes). Also TV/electronic device in bedroom. Direct quotes: 'Talking/texting on the phone... and using social media were all prospectively associated with shorter sleep duration, greater overall sleep disturbance.' Sleep outcomes are individual-trait-level (health, behavioral)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: OR=1.13 (95% CI 1.05–1.23) for talking/texting on sleep disturbance; B=–0.17 (95% CI –0.22, –0.12) for sleep duration; similar coefficients for social media and other modalities

FT_IC4.evidence: Prospective analysis with quantitative results: logistic regression OR values, linear regression B coefficients with 95% CI for all phone/SM and sleep outcome associations

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (Year 2→Year 3) but SM/phone is IV (predictor), not DV. Bedtime screen use predicts sleep outcomes; sleep is the DV. SM-as-IV analysis present, so EC7 does not apply
notes: Clear prospective cohort with bedtime-specific phone/SM measures (texting, social media) as IVs and sleep disturbance/duration as DVs. Individual-trait outcome (sleep health/behavior). EC7 inapplicable because SM is on left side of equation (IV).
